cu_txt_unit_wysiwyg.cs_txt_unit_wysiwyg: Sometimes, in order to a prove an arithmetical fact ∀xφ(x) by
induction, straightforward induction “does not work”. Instead one
“must” use a “stronger” induction hypothesis ψ(x) and prove ∀xψ(x),
from which ∀xφ(x) may be derived. This is sometimes
We show that this is an instance of a more general phenomenon
Cruacially, we show that there are cases
where straightforward induction fails, but where proof by
Sometimes, in order to a prove an arithmetical fact ∀xφ(x) by induction,
straightforward induction “does not work”. Instead one “must” use a
“stronger” induction hypothesis ψ(x) and prove ∀xψ(x), from which ∀xφ(x)
may be derived. This is sometimes called “proof by
To give an example, suppose we want to prove that, for all natural numbers
n, the sum of the first n odd numbers is a perfect square. Straightforward
the sum of the first 0 odd numbers is 0, which is a
if the sum of the first n odd numbers is a perfect
square k², then the sum of the first n + 1 odd numbers is
But it is not true that k² + 2n + 1 is a perfect square for all k
Instead, we may prove the following
for all natural numbers n, the sum of the first n odd numbers is n².
the sum of the 0 first odd numbers is 0, which is 0².
if the sum of the first n odd numbers is n², then
the sum of the first n + 1 odd numbers is n² + 2n + 1 = (n + 1)².
What does it mean, more precisely, to say that straightforward induction
to prove ∀xφ(x) “does not work”, and that we “must” use a “stronger”
to characterize the situation (where PA is Peano
In fact, this situation is impossible
yields PA ⊢ ∀xφ(x), which by pure logic yields
Instead, the situation may perhaps be characterized as follows
from the axioms of Peano arithmetic minus the induction axioms, we
successively prove more and more theorems using logic and the rule of
Suppose that, at some stage in this process of mathematical inquiry, we
have reached a theory T consisting of the axioms and hitherto proved
We say that a theory T and formulas φ(x) and ψ(x) exemplify a case of
There are extensions T of Robinson arithmetic and formulas φ(x) and ψ(x)
exemplifying cases of proof by non-straightforward induction.
Perhaps more surprisingly, proof by non-straightforward induction
does not really require ψ(x) to be
below, there are cases of proof by
relative to T, ψ(x) is actually
We define a non-standard model M of Robinson arithmetic as follows.
be a countably infinite set disjoint from the natural numbers ℕ, and let
the domain of the model be ℕ ∪ A.
We shall call A the (set of)

interpretation of the function symbols ', +, and × to the non-standard
for z ∈ ℤ and n ∈ ℕ.
for z ∈ ℤ and n ∈ ℕ − {0}.

, which is the theory you get by adding commutativity,
associativity and distribution laws for addition and multiplication to the

being satisfied by all natural numbers in the standard model as well as in
the non-standard model) with the following profile regarding the
φ(x) | ... false false true false false ...
ψ(x) | ... false false false false false ...

We say that a theory T and formulas φ(x) and ψ(x) exemplify a case of

There are extensions T of Robinson arithmetic and formulas φ(x) and ψ(x)
exemplifying a case of proof by incomparable induction hypothesis.
To show that proof by incomparable induction hypothesis is possible,
it suffices to find true formulas
φ(x) and ψ(x) with the following profile regarding the non-standard
φ(x) | ... true true true false false ...
ψ(x) | ... false false true true true ...
For instance, let φ(x) be the formula
∀y(x ≠ y ∧ ∃z(y + z = x) → x × x ≠ y × y)
and let ψ(x) be the formula
∀y(x ≠ y ∧ ∃z(x + z = y) → x × x ≠ y × y)
Observe that, by simple relabeling of variables, we have
Thus, let T be the theory you get by adding, to minimal arithmetic,
Clearly, both φ(x) and ψ(x) are true of all natural numbers in the
To verify that φ(x) has the required profile with respect to M,
we need to consider the following two cases

be true in M that x × x ≠ y × y. So φ(x) is true of the natural
x is assigned a non-standard number a
Then either y is assigned a natural number, in which case M
satisfies the antecedent x × x ≠ y × y, or y is assigned a
for some integer j ≠ i. By
satisfies x × x ≠ y × y. Likewise, whenever i > 0 and j = -i, M
satisfies ∃z(y + z = x) and x × x = y × y. Thus, φ(x) is true
in M for every non-standard number a
By symmetry of reasoning, we see that ψ(x) also has the required profile.

There are extensions T of Robinson arithmetic and formulas φ(x) and ψ(x)
exemplifying a case of proof by weakened induction hypothesis.
To show that proof by weakened induction hypothesis is possible,
formulas φ(x) and ψ(x) with the following profile
φ(x) | ... false false true false false ...
ψ(x) | ... false false true true true ...
For instance, let φ(x) be the formula
and let ψ(x) be the formula
∀y(x ≠ y ∧ ∃z(x + z = y) → x × x ≠ y × y)
Observe that ⊢ ∀x(φ(x) → ψ(x)). It should be clear from
Moreover, with u < v defined as
∀x∀y(x ≠ y ∧ ∃z(x + z = y) → x × x ≠ y × y)
, we have x < y or y < x.
In the case of x < y, we have by definition that ∃z(x + z = y). Thus, by
, we have x × x ≠ y × y, as desired.
In the case of y < x, we have by definition that ∃z(y + z = x).
, we get by relabeling of variables that
∀y∀x(y ≠ x ∧ ∃z(y + z = x) → y × y ≠ x × x)
, we get y × y ≠ x × x, which yields the desired result

Thus, let T be the theory you get by adding, to minimal arithmetic, the axioms
We here have cases where, relative to a base theory T, one
use one that is not stronger.
Are there cases where one both
This follows from a result reported by Hetzl and Wong
Proof by necessarily non-strengthened induction hypothesis
It can be shown that, if something is provable in an arithmetical theory containing all the
induction axioms, it is provable in that theory using at most one induction axiom.
This generalizes Corollary 2.10 reported by Hetzl and Wong.
For each formula Θ(x,z̄), we define the corresponding induction axiom IΘ as the closed formula

Generalizing Corollary 2.10 of Hetzl and Wong
D be the set of induction axioms, let T be a set of sentences,
and let σ be a sentence. If T + I
D ⊢ σ, then there is a formula φ(x) such
D ⊢ σ. Then there is a finite set
θ₁(x,z̄), ..., θₙ(x,z̄) of formulas such that T + Iθ₁ + ... + Iθₙ ⊢ σ.
, there is a formula φ(x) such that
⊢ Iφ ↔ Iθ₁ ∧ ... ∧ Iθₙ. Hence, T + Iφ ⊢ σ.
